title: What China Thinks It Can Gain From a Disordered World
gist: Oriana Skylar Mastro argues China's 30-year strategy is largely unchanged and even validated by global turmoil; a Taiwan invasion hinges on Beijing's own capabilities (which she thinks slip past 2027 to ~2028), and U.S. deterrence in Asia is a capability problem best solved by land-based missiles and Japanese day-one involvement, not drones.

themes.theme: China's net assessment and strategic continuity

insights.claim: China's grand strategy to become a great power has been essentially unchanged for 30 years and resistant to recent shocks because Beijing already predicted U.S. volatility, aggression, and risk-acceptance—so Trump's disruptions and the Iran and Venezuela wars merely confirm what Chinese planners expected.
insights.speaker: Oriana Skylar Mastro
insights.so_what: Warns against over-reading Trump-era chaos as changing China's trajectory—the strategic vector is stable.
insights.locator: Well, I think with Trump in office

insights.claim: China predicted the U.S. would turn much more aggressive once China's economy reached two-thirds the size of America's (which happened in the first Trump administration on a purchasing-power basis) and would become more risk-acceptant on the use of force.
insights.speaker: Oriana Skylar Mastro
insights.so_what: China's metric-driven net assessment means it reacts to measured power ratios, not headlines—useful for anticipating Beijing's posture.
insights.locator: And then they predicted

insights.claim: China recalibrated a few years ago from expecting to overtake the U.S. to expecting both to remain great powers 'on a different plane than everyone else,' growing in parallel rather than one surpassing the other.
insights.speaker: Oriana Skylar Mastro
insights.so_what: Beijing's confidence rests on parity, not dominance—reducing the odds of a desperate overreach.
insights.locator: So they did sort of recalibrate

insights.claim: Mastro judges China's self-assessment of power as broadly correct but says Beijing misdiagnoses the causes of its negative security environment in East Asia, blaming the U.S. rather than recognizing its own behavior drives neighbors' threat perceptions.
insights.speaker: Oriana Skylar Mastro
insights.so_what: This blind spot means China is unlikely to recalibrate its assertive behavior, sustaining regional tension.
insights.locator: So I think when it comes to assessing

insights.claim: The number one factor determining whether and when China invades Taiwan is Beijing's assessment of its own capabilities, not U.S. intervention threats, because the U.S. has not been effectively deterring China over Taiwan for a long time.
insights.speaker: Oriana Skylar Mastro
insights.so_what: Reframes the Taiwan timeline as a function of PLA readiness, not U.S. rhetoric—track capability, not statements.
insights.locator: As I wrote then, I still believe

insights.claim: China is only preparing for a fait accompli quick seizure of Taiwan of roughly two and a half to three weeks, and if it cannot do that fast it will not act short of something extreme like a Taiwan declaration of independence.
insights.speaker: Oriana Skylar Mastro
insights.so_what: The only scenario Beijing plans for is a rapid seizure—defenses that delay a landing beyond three weeks are the deterrent that matters.
insights.locator: Because there’s different types of contingencies

insights.claim: Mastro bluntly holds that Taiwan has zero chance of defending itself and can only hold out long enough for the U.S. to arrive, rejecting the 'self-defense' framing and the analogy to Ukraine holding off Russia.
insights.speaker: Oriana Skylar Mastro
insights.so_what: If correct, Taiwan-can-defend-itself assumptions underpinning some policy and investment theses are wrong; U.S. arrival is the decisive variable.
insights.quote: Taiwan has zero chance of defending itself.
insights.locator: And the United States can do very little

insights.claim: Mastro argues Xi's order for the military to be ready by 2027 was always a 'no earlier than' date, not a deadline, and China's command-and-control is not ready—Xi purged the vast majority of top PLA leaders over the past year—pushing a realistic invasion window to 2028.
insights.speaker: Oriana Skylar Mastro
insights.so_what: Counters the '2027 window' consensus—command-and-control gaps and purges buy more time than commonly assumed.
insights.locator: Now what has changed?

insights.claim: Chinese gray-zone actions and blockades against Taiwan will happen but are not reunification campaigns, because a blockade gives the U.S. time to amass forces in the region and makes a subsequent landing far more difficult.
insights.speaker: Oriana Skylar Mastro
insights.so_what: Blockade scenarios are punishment or battle-space prep, not the main invasion mode—recalibrates the threat taxonomy.
insights.locator: Yeah, I mean, I think those things

insights.claim: Taiwan is not comparable to Hong Kong because China physically controlled and had penetrated Hong Kong, whereas in Taiwan the population will not capitulate to Communist Party rule absent the actual use of force.
insights.speaker: Oriana Skylar Mastro
insights.so_what: Rules out a bloodless political absorption of Taiwan—force remains a prerequisite for reunification.
insights.locator: It’s very different from Hong Kong

insights.claim: Trump's unpredictability acts as a 'madman theory' that imposes caution on Beijing, because China's theory of victory over Taiwan rests on assuming a rational U.S. president would not launch full war after a fait accompli—an assumption Trump undermines.
insights.speaker: Oriana Skylar Mastro
insights.so_what: Trump's erratic reputation may paradoxically strengthen near-term Taiwan deterrence.
insights.locator: I think it does affect the calculus

insights.claim: Mastro finds it highly unlikely China uses force against Taiwan during the Trump administration, given both Beijing's unreadiness and its uncertainty about how Trump makes decisions.
insights.speaker: Oriana Skylar Mastro
insights.so_what: A relatively reassuring near-term base case for Taiwan-exposed assets through this U.S. term.
insights.locator: But given that they’re uncertain

insights.claim: China deliberately avoids traditional alliances because good relations with everybody are more effective—being fully behind Iran, North Korea, or Russia would cost China its ties with Israel, South Korea, and the EU, its largest trading partner.
insights.speaker: Oriana Skylar Mastro
insights.so_what: Explains why China hedges rather than commits—its partnerships are transactional, not mutual-defense.
insights.locator: And that is that China does not have traditional alliances

insights.claim: Mastro asserts that if forced to choose, China would pick Europe over Russia 'every single time' because the EU economic relationship matters far more to Beijing than what it gets from Moscow.
insights.speaker: Oriana Skylar Mastro
insights.so_what: Implies European economic leverage, if actually used, could force China to distance from Russia—a policy lever the EU underuses.
insights.quote: if China felt like they had to choose between Europe and Russia, they would choose Europe every single time
insights.locator: But one thing I say a lot in Europe

insights.claim: Great powers do not form mutual-defense alliances; they seek nonaggression pacts—wanting a partner to stay passive while they fight—and help building their own militaries, so the real impact of the China-Russia relationship is in peacetime, not wartime.
insights.speaker: Oriana Skylar Mastro
insights.so_what: Reframes the 'no-limits partnership' debate: worry less about joint war-fighting and more about how peacetime cooperation strengthens each military.
insights.locator: So I think that’s the wrong way of looking at it

insights.claim: The U.S. capture of Maduro and its close ties to Iran exposed that China has no ability and no desire to protect its partners from U.S. military intervention, which could push Beijing toward building overseas bases and power-projection if partners demand protection in exchange for trade.
insights.speaker: Oriana Skylar Mastro
insights.so_what: A shift toward Chinese expeditionary military capability would be a major structural change to watch as an early signal.
insights.locator: The problem with these conflicts

insights.claim: China's model of protecting overseas interests via police and law-enforcement cooperation assumed threats came from internal instability; now that the threat comes from external U.S. intervention, those mechanisms are invalidated.
insights.speaker: Oriana Skylar Mastro
insights.so_what: Explains why China may rethink how it secures its global economic footprint—potential driver of future military spending.
insights.locator: Another aspect of this is that China has relied mainly

insights.claim: Russia-North Korea closeness has significantly raised Korean Peninsula crisis risk: North Korean troops are gaining real combat experience in Ukraine—a senior U.S. Army three-star told Mastro they now have more relevant combined-arms experience than the U.S. Army—and are receiving Russian tech transfers like advanced satellite surveillance.
insights.speaker: Oriana Skylar Mastro
insights.so_what: North Korea's conventional capability is rising via battlefield experience and Russian tech—raising odds of a limited offensive against the South.
insights.locator: So I think it has significantly increased

insights.claim: North Korea now has a Russian alternative to sole dependence on China for energy, food, trade, and diplomatic protection, so China has lost leverage and has begun voting with Russia at the UN in support of Pyongyang rather than pressuring it.
insights.speaker: Oriana Skylar Mastro
insights.so_what: The collapse of China's constraining role removes a longstanding brake on North Korean behavior.
insights.locator: And then the second thing has to do

insights.claim: Mastro endorses Victor Cha's 'cold peace' approach—the U.S. should abandon denuclearization as the goal and pragmatically pursue an end-of-war agreement that contains rather than eliminates the North Korean nuclear problem.
insights.speaker: Oriana Skylar Mastro
insights.so_what: Signals a shifting expert consensus toward accepting a nuclear North Korea—reframing the diplomatic endgame.
insights.locator: I do. And it’s the same with ending the war in Ukraine

insights.claim: Mastro's priority for North Korea is capping delivery systems over denuclearization, because Pyongyang has not successfully tested a three-stage ICBM credibly reaching the U.S. mainland—a successful test would be a genuine game changer for deterrence.
insights.speaker: Oriana Skylar Mastro
insights.so_what: A successful North Korean three-stage ICBM test is the specific trip-wire that would transform the strategic picture—watch for tests.
insights.locator: So this is why the number one thing

insights.claim: In every major war game Mastro has joined, the U.S. and South Korea win a Korea war quickly (seven to ten days); the real problem is casualties and whether North Korea uses nuclear weapons, and she argues the artillery threat is overblown because analysts count tubes not launchers.
insights.speaker: Oriana Skylar Mastro
insights.so_what: Reframes Korea risk as a cost-imposition problem, not a who-wins problem—different from the Taiwan capability gap.
insights.locator: So the situation with a Korean war

insights.claim: China has a plan to secure 85 percent of North Korean nuclear weapons if it intervenes in the first days of a conflict, and Mastro argues the U.S. should not deter Chinese involvement even at the cost of less control over the war's end.
insights.speaker: Oriana Skylar Mastro
insights.so_what: A counterintuitive stance that Chinese intervention in a Korea war could serve U.S. nonproliferation interests.
insights.locator: But North Korea has nuclear weapons

insights.claim: Mastro says Taiwan deterrence is a capability problem, not a resolve problem, and the fix is land-based intermediate-range ballistic missiles—positioned far enough from Chinese threat rings to survive—which the U.S. could not build under a treaty until 2017 and still has not fielded nine years later.
insights.speaker: Oriana Skylar Mastro
insights.so_what: Names the single capability she believes would 'fundamentally solve' Taiwan deterrence—a procurement gap to watch.
insights.locator: Land-based intermediate range ballistic missiles

insights.claim: Mastro argues unmanned systems and drones are 'a way to lose the war more cheaply' over the Taiwan Strait, because air defenses shoot down aircraft and drones alike and there is nowhere survivable to base thousands of them—access, basing, and overflight is the number one Indo-Pacific problem.
insights.speaker: Oriana Skylar Mastro
insights.so_what: A sharp dissent from the drone-centric consensus for Taiwan—basing geography, not drone quantity, is the binding constraint.
insights.locator: My own view is that that’s just a way to lose the war

insights.claim: Japanese day-one offensive involvement would be enough to deter China because Japan can bring mass into theater in two days versus three weeks for U.S. submarines, which explains the ferocity of China's response to PM Takaichi's statements on Taiwan.
insights.speaker: Oriana Skylar Mastro
insights.so_what: Japan's early commitment is the highest-leverage deterrence variable—watch Tokyo's posture and China's reactions to it.
insights.locator: If the Japanese fought alongside the United States

insights.claim: Mastro contends U.S. bases in the Philippines and Japan would not be struck on day one because that would bring the U.S. fully into the war, so a 'sanctuary period' of a couple of weeks is likely and survivability fears should not block building forward force posture.
insights.speaker: Oriana Skylar Mastro
insights.so_what: Challenges the survivability objection used to justify under-investing in forward Indo-Pacific bases.
insights.locator: So a lot of these concerns that prevent us

insights.claim: Mastro warns that U.S. allies Japan, Australia, and South Korea drawing closer together without U.S. backing may signal alliance atrophy and weakened deterrence rather than strength, because China's number one strategic goal is to break up the alliances and prefers allies that coordinate but are alienated from Washington.
insights.speaker: Oriana Skylar Mastro
insights.so_what: A warning that apparent allied cohesion may mask a deterrence-eroding decoupling from U.S. leadership.
insights.locator: But I worry that it’s a sign of the atrophy

insights.claim: Mastro notes that across Graham Allison's cases of rising powers, over 80 percent of the time the rising power overtakes the reigning great power—whether through war or peacefully—though she holds out U.S. exceptionalism as the possible 20 percent.
insights.speaker: Oriana Skylar Mastro
insights.so_what: Base rates favor China overtaking the U.S.—a sobering prior against complacency.
insights.locator: Not only does the tortoise win that race

insights.claim: China deliberately built its power in ways designed to avoid triggering a U.S. 'Sputnik moment,' but Mastro believes the U.S.—still the largest economy and energy independent—retains runway to get its act together, as it tends to do at the last possible minute.
insights.speaker: Oriana Skylar Mastro
insights.so_what: A late-mobilization U.S. wake-up remains possible and would be very problematic for China's timeline.
insights.locator: So China’s strategy has worked so far
